checks.a_analysis: I have carefully compared the text content of each section in the output with the corresponding pages in the source PDF. The output accurately reflects the meeting agenda, minutes, and presentation slides. The conversion of the attendance table in the minutes into a summary paragraph is an acceptable transformation of the source data. No invented headings or body text were found.

The document contains dates, location names (e.g., Microsoft Teams Meeting), and a list of URLs on slide 13. The dates and meeting information match the source. The URLs on slide 13 are represented as an image in the output, which is acceptable. The statistical data and counts (e.g., 1,726 Total Clicks) on slide 14 match the source exactly. No phone numbers or emails were present in the source to verify.

The source document consists of 18 pages (an agenda, three pages of minutes, and 14 slides). All sections corresponding to these pages are present in the output document, from the initial agenda to the final 'Next Steps' slide.

On page 8 of the source, there is a list titled 'Zone Cities and Zips' containing two columns of 20 rows each (totaling 40 data points). The output attempts to format this data as a series of ten small tables ('Zone Cities and Zips Table 1' through 'Table 10'). Collectively, these tables contain only about 10 rows of data, meaning significantly more than 20% of the structured rows are missing from the table elements. While the information is mostly preserved in a preceding paragraph, the table structures themselves are a major failure of content fidelity.
checks.b_evidence_source: The 'Zone Cities and Zips' list on page 8 contains 20 rows of city/zip pairs in a two-column format (40 entries total).
checks.b_evidence_output: The ten 'Zone Cities and Zips' tables in Section 6 contain approximately 10 data rows in total, omitting the vast majority of the structured entries found in the source.

checks.check_name: Significant text omission within a section
checks.a_analysis: Despite the failure of the table formatting in Section 6 (Page 8), the actual text of the 'Zone Cities and Zips' list is included in its entirety within a paragraph preceding the broken tables. Other sections, including the multi-page minutes and complex data slides, appear to be captured in full without significant text omissions.

checks.check_name: Minor text differences beyond acceptable reformatting
checks.a_analysis: A minor text discrepancy was found in the fragmented 'Zone Cities and Zips' tables in Section 6. In 'Table 9', the zip code '27589' is associated with the city 'Stem'. However, the source document correctly identifies 'Stem' with zip code '27581'. This change alters the meaning of the data.
checks.b_evidence_source: The list on page 8 correctly pairs '27581' with 'Stem'.
checks.b_evidence_output: Zone Cities and Zips Table 9 incorrectly pairs zip code '27589' with 'Stem'.
checks.b_evidence_location: Section 6, Page 8, Table element 'Zone Cities and Zips Table 9'

checks.a_analysis: The data from the 'Zone Cities and Zips' section on page 8 is duplicated in the output. The content appears first as a long text string in a paragraph element at the beginning of Section 6, and is then immediately repeated across ten fragmented table elements (e.g., 'Zone Cities and Zips Table 1', 'Table 2', etc.) within the same section.
checks.b_evidence_source: The list of zip codes and cities on Page 8 (e.g., '27231 Cedar Grove', '27278 Hillsborough', '27882 Spring Hope').
checks.b_evidence_output: The text appears in the paragraph starting 'Zone Cities and Zips 27231 Cedar Grove...' and again in the cells of the tables that follow.

checks.a_analysis: The heading hierarchy is flat but consistent. Major document sections and individual slide titles are assigned H2 levels. This is a common accessibility normalization and does not skip levels (e.g., H1 to H3).

The multi-column grid of 'Zone Cities and Zips' on page 8 is severely corrupted. Instead of a single cohesive table or list, the pipeline fragmented it into ten separate 1-row tables. Furthermore, the column alignment is broken; for instance, 'Zone Cities and Zips Table 1' uses three headers ('Zip', 'City', 'City') for a two-column source layout, incorrectly merging a zip code and city name into a single cell (e.g., '27882 Spring Hope'). Additionally, several tables from the presentation (Pages 12, 13) were not extracted as structural tables at all, remaining only as images.
checks.b_evidence_source: The 4-column grid of Zip codes and Cities on page 8.
checks.b_evidence_output: The series of fragmented tables in Section 6, specifically 'Zone Cities and Zips Table 1' with headers [Zip, City, City] and content [27278, Hillsborough, 27882 Spring Hope].

checks.a_analysis: The reading order of the output follows the logical flow of the source document, progressing from the agenda to the minutes and then through the presentation slides. Minor reordering of the 'Next Meeting' footer on Page 1 to the top of Section 1 is an acceptable accessibility adjustment.
Nested lists are handled correctly. For example, on page 14 (Slide 10), the sub-bullets under 'Municipalities' URLS accessed inclusive of:' are properly nested in the JSON structure.

The output fails to extract the list of government URLs from the 'Top 10 Clicks' table on page 13. While the table is visible in the source, it is only provided as an image in the JSON output (Section 11), meaning all functional links are missing from the document's structural text.
checks.b_evidence_source: The list of URLs on Page 13 (e.g., https://durhamnc.gov/3774/City-Jobs-Careers).
checks.b_evidence_output: The absence of text or link elements in Section 11, which only contains an image.

checks.a_analysis: The converted HTML maintains the overall organizational structure of the source PDF. The document begins with a Table of Contents, followed by the February 17, 2022 Agenda, the September 23, 2021 Minutes (including attendee and vote tables), and finally the presentation slides for the Recruitment PSA Campaign. While many images are missing (addressed in V3), the visual flow and sequence of information matches the source's intent.

Tables across the document are generally well-rendered. The attendee tables (page 2), vote tables (page 4), and the 'PSAP Impacts' data table (page 16) are all rendered with clear rows and columns that are easy for a sighted user to read and understand. The ZIP code list on page 8, which appeared as a multi-column table in the PDF, has been rendered as a series of smaller tables in the HTML, which is an acceptable responsive layout change and does not hinder readability.

The HTML output fails to render numerous meaningful images that constitute the core content of the presentation slides. Specifically, the map of the Durham Zone (page 8), the TV network logos for 'Campaign Airings' (page 9), and the screenshots of the Google Analytics dashboards (pages 10, 11, 12, and 13) are all missing. In the rendered HTML, these are replaced by large green rectangular placeholders, leaving the document without its primary data visualizations.
checks.b_evidence_source: The source PDF contains a map on page 8, network logos on page 9, and Google Analytics dashboard screenshots on pages 10, 11, 12, and 13.
checks.b_evidence_output: In the HTML, these sections (e.g., under the heading 'Webpage Analytics: Site Summary') contain only large green boxes instead of the images.
checks.b_evidence_location: Presentation slides section, corresponding to PDF pages 8, 9, 10, 11, 12, and 13.

checks.a_analysis: A thorough visual inspection of the rendered HTML does not reveal any instances of body content (paragraphs, tables, or list items) being duplicated. The repetition of headings in the Table of Contents and the body is expected accessible design.
